body.title: [sprint] Skills carry an explicit greppable Why: (the format the auditor keys on)
body.description:
**Problem:** Skills (and their MANDATORY steps) don't uniformly carry an explicit why. The why is what turns a brittle lookup into a generative rule, is the conflict tiebreaker, and is the only lever that rescues unprompted recognition. Without it, skill instructions are followed only when salient and cannot be drift-checked.
**Proposed shape:** Document the contract in the skill-authoring location (CLAUDE.md skill section / skills authoring guide): every skill carries a top-level why, and each step marked MANDATORY carries a one-line why. Define a MACHINE-DETECTABLE format (a `Why:` line) so the auditor (T-skill-why-auditor) can key on presence/structure.
**Affected files:** skill-authoring doc (convention text); a sample skill annotated to the convention. No code-behavior change.
**Design rules:** Theory-of-Operation (every rule carries its why); External State Principle (the why is durable, not in-context).
**Scope boundary:** IN — the documented convention + the greppable `Why:` format. OUT — backfilling existing skills (that is the auditor's first run); enforcement tooling.
**Completion criteria:** A discoverable written convention stating skills + MANDATORY steps carry an explicit `Why:`; the format is greppable; it is referenced from the skill-authoring location.
**Test plan:** No tests (convention/docs). Verify a sample skill annotated to the convention is matched by a grep for the `Why:` format.
